17 min read and 6 min video
In Part I of the Audience and Offer Masterclass, I identi�ed three
overlapping areas to consider when identifying an offer to bring
to an audience.
▪ The creator’s interests, experience, and/or abilities;
▪ An audience that has a need for the outcomes created by
those elements; and
▪ A clearly articulated demonstration of the value — to the
audience — those elements can create.
As a result of that exercise, I hope that you are beginning to
identify opportunities to create value for a speci�c audience in
ways that are bene�cial to them and interesting to you.
The goal of that exercise is not to �nd the ‘right’ answer. It’s a
method for discovering ideas that resonate with you. You may
have con�rmed you’re already on the right track, or you may have
uncovered perspectives you hadn’t seen before. Return to that
process whenever you feel stuck. It’s a reliable place to begin.
Audience & Offer Masterclass
(Part II)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 25 --

For the second part of this masterclass, I’m going to assume that
you’ve done the work in part I — that you already have an offer,
or that you are working with others’ offers (either in a client-
services relationship or as an af�liate). An approximate idea for a
product or service, as well as a core audience for that product or
service, are helpful prerequisites for getting the most value from
part II of this masterclass.
If you don’t have one or both of those yet, read through Part II. It
may spark some ideas.
Several years ago I was introduced to the phrase “framework
before work” by one of my employees, and that idea has stayed
with me ever since. By my de�nition, a framework is a
mechanism for focusing on the few things that matter most to
produce an important result within any given topic.
For example, Sphere of In�uence is a framework for
understanding customer acquisition. The Art of Email is a
framework for building relationships via email. Neither provides
templates to follow, but each provides a methodology that
produces superior results.
Ideally, frameworks provide enough guidance to be broadly
useful, but are not so restrictive that they inhibit creativity.
The remainder of this masterclass will focus on the most effective
framework I’ve ever used for understanding audiences and
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 25 --

offers. I have used it with clients, used it for myself, and taught it
to college students.
Without exception, feedback and results have been positive.
This framework begins with a crucial concept that may be
dif�cult to understand at �rst. To make that easier for you to
grasp and internalize, I’ll use a (somewhat embarrassing) example
from my own experience to make that concept more accessible.
Several years ago I paid more than $300 for a watch. For many of
you that may not be remarkable. For me it was because I am not a
‘watch guy’. In fact, I rarely wear a watch.
Why a $300 watch? Your �rst thought might be that the features
really mattered to me. This particular watch has an altimeter,
barometer, and compass. Well … I assume it does. I’ve never used
any of those functions.
I know what you’re thinking — who would buy a $300 watch and
not use any of its features?
Before I answer that question I’ll share one more detail — the
digital display of the watch is very dif�cult to read. I had LASIK
eye surgery nine years ago, and I’m forty-nine. I joke that I can see
the International Space Station from my deck, but I can’t read a
menu (or my watch) right in front of me.
Let’s recap — I paid $300 for a watch that I can barely read that has
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 25 --

features I never use. What was I thinking?
Let’s step back and ask a better question. Why would someone pay
$300 for a watch that’s dif�cult to read and has features he has no
intention of using?
That’s a really great question, and it points the way to a profound
insight about buying behavior.
The watch in question is a Suunto Core Military — All Black.
(They are considerably less expensive now if you’re interested in
buying one.) For months the watch was impossible to �nd
because it was featured in one of my favorite movies — The
Equalizer with Denzel Washington.
The watch appears during the turning point of the movie when
Robert McCall, played by Denzel Washington, attempts to buy the
freedom of a teenage prostitute he has recently befriended. When
his offer of $9,800 is rejected by Russian ma�a boss Slavi, McCall
starts to leave the room, stops at the door, opens and closes it
repeatedly, and then locks the door and turns to face a room full of
bad guys.
We then see McCall — a former Marine, intelligence operative,
and assassin who faked his own death to leave that career behind
— look at everyone in the room in slow-motion detail before he
says aloud, to himself, “16 seconds…”
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 25 --

That’s his estimate for how long it’ll take him to kill everyone in
the room. Then, McCall raises his arm, starts the stopwatch of his
Suunto Core Military — All Black watch, and proceeds to wreak
havoc on everyone in the room.
(You can see the full scene here, just be aware that it’s violent and
graphic.)
So returning to our original question, why did I buy the watch?
Because it reminds me of a movie I really enjoyed based on an
archetypal theme that resonates deeply with me. When I wear the
watch I’m reminded of that theme (even if I can’t see what time it
is).
For me the watch is a talisman, and its value has nothing to do
with any of the functions it has.
The insight here, which will be critical for our understanding of
audiences and offers, is that people buy things for many
reasons that are rarely obvious. Yes, we do buy for functional
purposes, but we also buy for social and emotional reasons.
Understanding the full spectrum of reasons someone might buy
what you have to offer, beyond the obvious functional bene�ts, is
very powerful.
This insight about buying behavior is associated with Jobs to Be
Done Theory (JTBD) and is most closely associated with its
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 25 --

creator, the late Harvard University business professor and
management consultant Clayton Christensen. In his seminal
Harvard Business Review article entitled Marketing Malpractice:
The Cause and the Cure, Christensen and his co-authors
explained the idea this way:
“With few exceptions, every job people need or want to do has a
social, a functional, and an emotional dimension. If marketers
understand each of these dimensions, then they can design a product
that’s precisely targeted to the job. In other words, the job, not the
customer, is the fundamental unit of analysis for a marketer who hopes
to develop products that customers will buy.”
Unfortunately, discussions of JTBD have turned into intellectual
hair-splitting and polarization. We’re going to ignore all of that
and focus on one critical insight that we can use — purchasing
decisions have functional, emotional, and social
components.
In my watch example above, the purchasing decision had nothing
to do with function, and everything to do with emotion. None of
the features of the watch matter to me (I can’t even use it to tell
what time it is unless the lighting is perfect).
Take a moment to think about the last 5-10 purchases you’ve
made, big and small. How many of them were purely functional?
Which purchases had an emotional component? Social? (If you
doubt the social element of purchases, take a quick look at your
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 25 --

Facebook news feed and you’ll see evidence of that everywhere.)
I made that brief introduction to Jobs to Be Done Theory because
it’s a critical step in the framework we’ll use to create offers — the
Value Proposition Design Canvas (VPDC). In my experience,
this is the most effective framework to identify the most powerful
needs within an audience and match those needs precisely to the
product or service that you offer.
Value Proposition Design, and the way I use it, differs slightly
from Christensen’s approach. Instead of focusing only on jobs and
not people, we’re going to start with the audience we seek to
serve, and then identify the jobs they need to accomplish, gains
they hope to achieve, and pains they hope to avoid.
Then, we’ll specify a product or service we have created (or intend
to create), and identify the gain creators and pain relievers
associated with that product or service.
This probably sounds confusing. Here’s a quick screencast that
explains the basic framework.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 25 --

The visual in the screencast is a paid tool from Strategyzer. I want
to be very clear that you don’t need to use that tool (it’s expensive).
I’ll use it for demonstration purposes, but it is absolutely not
necessary. Paper, white boards, and sticky notes work very well
too. Don’t worry about the mechanism — it’s how we use it that
matters.
Here’s an overview of the process so you see the 10,000′
perspective before we get into the details and examples.
Step 1 — Identify a core audience that is a reasonably good �t for
the product or service you offer, or an idea you’d like to create (or
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 25 --

test).
Step 2 — Brainstorm that audience’s functional, social, and
emotional ‘jobs to be done’ as they relate to our product(s) or
service(s). What is your prospective customer trying to
accomplish, broadly speaking?
Step 3 — Brainstorm the ‘pains’ that prevent your audience from
accomplishing those jobs, risks associated with performing those
jobs, or factors that annoy them along the way.
Step 4 — Brainstorm the ‘gains’ your audience hopes to achieve
from accomplishing those jobs successfully. How will their lives
be better?
Steps 1 — 4 are captured on the right side of the Value
Proposition Design Canvas. You’ll want to complete this part of
the exercise for every individual audience you advertise to.
The best way to identify audiences is through this exercise. When
you �nd that members within your audience have different
priorities for their jobs, pains, and gains, that’s a clue to create a
separate audience.
Start with one audience and go deep. Spend the time to ask and
answer questions (you’ll be provided with prompts later). Don’t try
to do this work as quickly as possible. Instead, aim to be as
thorough as is valuable. You’re looking for insights, not quick
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 25 --

wins.
Step 5 — Identify your product, service, or idea. I recommend one
canvas per product/service/idea, unless there’s a very speci�c
reason to include more than one together.
Step 6 — List your product or service’s ‘pain relievers’. How,
speci�cally, do you help your customer save time, save money,
minimize risk, etc.?
Step 7 — List your product or service’s ‘gain creators’. How,
speci�cally, does your product or service create the outcomes and
bene�ts your customers want most?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 25 --

Strategyzer.com is an excellent resource for Value Proposition
Canvas Design. I highly recommend that you create a free
account, and download and review many of their materials.
Speci�cally, after you have created your account, choose
Resources (left side of the screen), and scroll down to the section
titled Value Proposition Supporting Tools.
In particular, I recommend all of the trigger questions documents
to use as prompts for each section of the canvas. You may �nd
other resources useful as well.
▪ The Value Proposition Canvas (PDF)
▪ Customer Gains Trigger Questions (PDF)
▪ Customer Jobs Trigger Questions (PDF)
▪ Customer Pains Trigger Questions (PDF)
▪ Gain Creators Trigger Questions (PDF)
▪ Pain Relievers Trigger Questions (PDF)
Now let’s discuss how we’ll use the Value Proposition Design
Canvas framework to create a high-performance offer from an
existing product or service, or an idea we would like to turn into
reality.
We’ll start by loosely de�ning our ideal audience. Who are your
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 25 --

best customers?
▪ Are they in a speci�c �eld, or are they in different �elds but
have the same general responsibilities?
▪ Are they primarily male or female?
▪ Is age a factor?
▪ Do they live primarily in urban or rural areas (or both)?
▪ Do they gravitate towards a particular political af�liation?
▪ Who do they rely on for buying-advice?
▪ Are they married or single?
Add any factors that are relevant to your product or service, and
then spend some time answering each thoughtfully. We want an
audience that’s broad enough to be useful for our business,
but not so broad that the speci�c needs of the audience are
too diverse. The goal of this �rst step is to have a clear idea of
who we are imagining as we complete our Value Proposition
Canvas.
For the remainder of this exercise we’ll be imagining the audience
we have identi�ed. As you go through the next three steps (jobs,
pains, and gains), think about your answers from the perspective
of this ideal audience.
Let’s start with their ‘jobs’:
www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 25 --

▪ What, speci�cally, is your
audience trying to accomplish?
▪ If you were to watch someone use
your product and service
successfully, what would s/he have
accomplished?
▪ What would the doing look like?
Now dig a little deeper:
▪ Why might someone want to accomplish that?
▪ What emotional needs are being met?
▪ How does someone feel using your product or service?
▪ How does someone feel buying your product or service?
▪ What does owning your product or using your service say to
others about that person?
For example, what are you saying to others when you post the
picture of your brand new Toyota Prius on Facebook? Or your
brand new Porsche 911?
What are you saying to yourself, and others, when you buy a
Rolex instead of a Timex? Or a Timex instead of a Rolex? (This
isn’t about judgment — it’s about understanding.)
Take some time with this step and capture whatever reveals itself.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 25 --

I recommend using sticky notes (Post-it notes here in the U.S.).
One note per idea, write concisely with bold ink so it’s visible, and
then add the notes to your whiteboard in the ‘Jobs to be Done’
section on the right side.
Your canvas will look something like this:
Next, repeat the same process for ‘pains’ that your audience
experiences when they’re trying to perform these jobs. Think
broadly about anything that prevents them from accomplishing
any of the jobs you have identi�ed.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 25 --

▪ What annoys them, slows things down (or speeds things
up)?
▪ What risks would they like to avoid?
▪ What are some of the potentially bad outcomes they’d like to
avoid?
Again, one sticky note per idea. Keep your ideas concise and
focused.
Remember, you’re answering each of these questions from the
perspective of the customer. For example, a customer job might be
getting an oil change for his/her car, and a pain associated with
that might be wasting time waiting at the service center. That’s
the type of relationship we’re looking for between jobs and pains.
Up next are gains:
www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 25 --

▪ From the customer’s perspective, what outcomes and
bene�ts are they looking for?
▪ Which ones are expected?
▪ Which might be a surprise?
Using the example above, a customer would expect their oil and
oil �lter to be changed, and they might be pleasantly surprised if
their car windows were washed inside and out too.
It’s important that the gains are something a customer
would want relative to the jobs you have identi�ed.
Now your canvas will look something like this:
If you’re struggling to think of questions, I recommend that you
use the prompts from Strategyzer mentioned earlier. You don’t
www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 25 --

need to answer every question — skim through the options to see
what works best for you, then capture your ideas.
One note before we proceed. You will be tempted to do this
exercise as lists (analog or digitally), instead of going through the
effort of capturing the ideas on individual notes and placing them
on the canvas. Don’t. You want to interact with these ideas as
much as possible, see them visually, and rearrange the notes. Your
experience, and your results, will be better if you take the time to
do this exercise the way I’m describing it.
Now we’re going to move on to the far left of the canvas. In the
Products & Services section, create a note for one product, one
service, or one idea. This is not your offer yet — this is a product
or service you sell (or a product or service you intend to sell).
Next, create individual notes for all of the ‘pain relievers’ that
your product, service, or idea creates for your customers. How,
exactly, does your product or service alleviate (or prevent)
problems for your customers before, during, or after they
complete the ‘job’ they’ve ‘hired’ you to do?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 17 of 25 --

▪ Have you saved them time, money, or effort?
▪ Is your product or service easier to use? How?
▪ Have you eliminated any risks they usually face when using
a similar product?
When you’re answering these questions, think beyond the
obvious and include social and emotional components as well. For
example, if you teach people how to dance in the privacy of their
home, you’re relieving the ‘pain’ of social embarrassment (not
being able to dance), and the ‘pain’ of feeling like a fool learning in
a classroom setting with others. Those are the deeper insights
you’re looking for.
Finally, brainstorm and capture ideas for gain creators:
▪ How does your product, service, or idea produce bene�t for
your customers?
▪ What outcomes do you provide that your customer needs or
www.getwsodo.com

-- 18 of 25 --

wants?
▪ What else might they want they haven’t thought to ask?
▪ How, speci�cally, does your product, service, or idea provide
value (functionally, socially, and emotionally)?
Your complete Value Proposition Design Canvas will now look
something like this:
At this point in the process you might be overwhelmed. That’s OK,
and it’s perfectly natural. We’re going to �x that soon.
Let’s take a step back and see what we’ve accomplished. We have
identi�ed:
▪ An audience.
▪ The various ‘jobs’ that audience needs to accomplish as they
www.getwsodo.com

-- 19 of 25 --

relate to our product, service, or idea.
▪ Their pains associated with accomplishing those jobs.
▪ Their gains associated with accomplishing those jobs.
▪ Our product, service, or idea.
▪ The ways in which our product, service, or idea relieves
pains for our customers.
▪ The ways in which our product, service, or idea creates
gains for our customers.
That seems like a LOT, and it is, but there’s another critically
important step that makes this so much more powerful.
We are not trying to be all things to everybody. We do not want to
overwhelm our audience with every problem they’re trying to
solve or every outcome they seek to achieve.
Instead, what we’re looking for are the few things that really
matter from our customer’s perspective.
▪ What 1-3 jobs are the most important? These are the things
that really, really, really matter to the customer.
▪ Then, what are the 1-3 pains that cause the most problems?
▪ And the 1-3 gains that create the most opportunity and joy?
If you’ve been using sticky notes, remove those few notes from
the canvas (leave everything else as is). Convert those notes into a
www.getwsodo.com

-- 20 of 25 --

digital list and congratulate yourself — you’ve just identi�ed
the critical few things that matter most to the audience you
identi�ed for this exercise. That is an amazing
accomplishment.
Now, we’re going to complete the exercise.
For each of the jobs, pains, and gains you have identi�ed, �nd
your product, service, or idea’s relevant pain relievers and gain
creators. How, speci�cally, do you prevent or address your
customer’s most impactful pains?
How, speci�cally, do you provide your customers with their most
desired outcomes?
Let me be very clear — we are not trying to match every customer
pain and every customer gain with a pain reliever and gain
creator. Instead, we are identifying the few jobs, pains, and
gains that really matter to our audience, and then explaining
exactly how our product, service, or idea addresses what matters
most to them.
I recommend that you capture all of this information digitally
from this point forward (in a single document). This document
will become your messaging brief for offers and advertising. The
format should look something like this:
Audience Description. Include all of the relevant details you
www.getwsodo.com

-- 21 of 25 --

identi�ed when you imagined this audience.
Most Important Jobs to Be Done (1-3). Describe each in detail.
Most Important Customer Pains (1-3). Describe each in detail.
Most Important Customer Gains (1-3). Describe each in detail.
Pain Relievers. How, speci�cally, does your product, service, or
idea prevent, minimize, or solve the customer’s most important
pains?
Gain Creators. How, speci�cally, does your product, service, or
idea create the most important opportunities, bene�ts, or
outcomes that your customers want most?
This document is your strategic messaging brief.
It focuses your attention on what you offer to a speci�c audience.
It describes what matters to that audience as it relates to what you
have to offer. It’s concise, and if you’ve done your work well you
have focused on the few things that really matter.
Once you have completed and captured this work you will refer to
this document when you write your landing pages, sales pages,
emails, and ads. It is a method for continuously re-orienting to
what matters most to your audience.
After you’ve completed this exercise once and used what you have
www.getwsodo.com

-- 22 of 25 --

compiled to build assets for that audience, you have a couple of
options.
First, you can verify that your assumptions were accurate. How
you do that will depend on your speci�c business. You might ask
for feedback and verify that the things that you think matter
actually matter most.
Or you can measure results and assume that great results mean
you were on the right track and poor results mean you need to go
back to the drawing board and look at other jobs, pains and gains
instead.
Second, you can repeat the process from start to �nish for another
audience. If the jobs, pains, and gains are different then you’ll
want to communicate with that audience differently. Earlier I
mentioned that an audience is best de�ned by the combination of
jobs, pains, and gains that resonate most, and that may take some
research and testing.
For example, several years ago I worked with a client that did this
exercise with one audience. However, it became clear very quickly
that different jobs, different pains, and different gains mattered to
people in that audience. We couldn’t create a value proposition
that would stand out until we realized that they actually had three
separate audiences.
Each had something in common that attracted them to the
www.getwsodo.com

-- 23 of 25 --

product, but the way they used the product meant that different
pain relievers and different gain creators mattered.
One audience used the product for themselves. Another used it for
a single client (in an employer/employee relationship). A third
audience used it in an agency-client relationship. Each audience
had different jobs, pains, and gains that mattered most, and we
were able to tailor messaging for each audience accordingly.
The audience that used the product for their own business didn’t
care about the slick reports that the audience with clients relied
on, for example. And the audience that worked on behalf of a
single client appreciated the ROI-speci�c reporting detail that
could be shared with the boss, which served functional,
emotional, and social needs.
I want to be very clear that you will get no bene�t from this
module if you don’t do the work, or if you rush through the work
to check the box and move on.
Use this framework. Download the free resources. Think deeply
about the questions (mine and the downloadable prompts). If you
do this work you will understand your audiences’ needs so much
better than if you try to guess, or compile information
haphazardly.
That is not hyperbole. I’ve seen paid advertising campaigns
transformed by this process. I’ve also seen two of my college
www.getwsodo.com

-- 24 of 25 --

students use it as the foundation for an offer they created that
won a state-wide business pitch competition. They did the work
and got an amazing result. You can too.
— Shawn
www.getwsodo.com

-- 25 of 25 --
